schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'he finished a palace' (cf. 1 Chronicles 14:1), the city of David as the castle of Zion; Keil notes the Chronicler elaborates the religious side that Samuel omits, the tent on Zion while the Mosaic tabernacle stayed at Gibeon.
Wesley: a palace of many houses for David's wives and children, and no Mosaic tabernacle fetched from Gibeon because he meant to build the temple; Clarke: one house for himself, one for the ark.
Matthew Henry makes the prepared place a lesson: David sought God in the due order, and wherever we build for ourselves we must make room for God's ark.
Gill takes the houses for the royal palace raised with Hiram's masons and carpenters.

themes: preparing a place for the ark

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: 'then' marks the end of the three months; the Levites alone may bear the ark (Numbers 4, 7; Deut. 10:8), and David's ruling tacitly confesses the cart was contrary to law, though Samuel notes bearers without calling them Levites.
Clarke: it was the Levites' business; had David thought of it sooner the breach upon Uzza would have been prevented (v.13).
Gill notes only that David said it, within himself or to his courtiers.

themes: Levites alone may bear the ark

schools.summary: Keil identifies 'all Israel' as elders, rulers of thousands, and heads of families (cf. 2 Samuel 6:15), and explains Kohath's precedence; Ellicott notes Samuel omits Jerusalem and that 'unto his place' treats the ark almost as a person.
Gill takes 'all Israel' for the chief and heads of the people, not every individual.

themes: the order of the Levite houses

schools.summary: Ellicott names the children of Aaron as Zadok and Abiathar and the six Levite chiefs, and explains the four Kohathite houses against one each of Merari and Gershom by Kohath's charge of the ark (Numbers 4:4).
Gill: David assembled the priests and Levites whose business was to bear the ark; from here begins the account of their number.
Ellicott and Keil set Kohath first as the senior clan to which Aaron's house belonged, charged with the ark; Uriel the chief, 120 brethren.
Wesley traces the sons of Kohath through Amram and Izhar (Numbers 3:27), noting Elizaphan, Hebron, and Uzziel (vv.8-10) are also Kohathite houses.

themes: Uriel and his hundred and twenty

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott set Joel at the head of the Gershomites, the house of Gershon, Levi's firstborn son (Exodus 6:16), with 130 brethren — the eldest branch listed after Kohath because place is by appointment, not birth.

themes: the firstborn's house set after Kohath

schools.summary: Ellicott: Elizaphan son of Uzziel, fourth son of Kohath (Exodus 6:18, 22); Shemaiah chief in David's day (1 Chronicles 24:6); Keil counts 200 brethren, the largest company yet.
Wesley reckons Elizaphan among the children of Kohath, so this house too belongs to the senior clan whose charge was the ark.
Ellicott: Hebron the third son of Kohath (Exodus 6:18; 1 Chronicles 6:2); Keil gives Eliel the chief and fourscore brethren, the smallest company of the six.
Wesley numbers Hebron among the children of Kohath, so this house too belongs to the ark's clan.

Ellicott: Uzziel the fourth son of Kohath (1 Chronicles 6:2; Exodus 6:22 names Mishael, Elzaphan, Zithri); since Elzaphan's house already appeared, 'sons of Uzziel' means the two remaining stocks, perhaps amalgamated (1 Chronicles 23:20; 24:24); Keil gives Amminadab and 112.
Wesley counts Uzziel among the children of Kohath, closing the senior clan.

themes: Amminadab and a hundred and twelve

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil make Zadok and Abiathar coordinate heads of the lines of Eleazar and Ithamar, both holding the high priesthood (1 Chronicles 6:4; 24:3; 2 Samuel 15:24; 20:25); Ellicott reads the leaders' names as a confession that Jehovah is just, alone God, and hears prayer.
Wesley makes Abiathar the high priest and Zadok the second priest — a chief and his deputy, not equals.
Gill notes only that David called for the two priests.

debates.question: What was the relative rank of Zadok and Abiathar?

sides.position: coordinate heads of the two priestly lines (Eleazar and Ithamar), both holding the high priesthood
Abiathar the high priest and Zadok the second priest

themes: the meaning of the leaders' names

schools.summary: Ellicott expounds 'sanctify yourselves' as the prescribed purifications (Genesis 35:2; Exodus 19:10; 30:17; 2 Chronicles 30:3) and hears in 'the ark of the LORD God of Israel' a solemn, national title (Exodus 19:6; 1 Samuel 2:30); Keil ties the charge to the breach upon Uzza.
Gill: 'the chief of the fathers' are the heads of the Levites' fathers' houses.

themes: the solemn title of the ark

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'it was not you' (the Levite chiefs) but Uzza and Ahio who carried the ark (2 Samuel 6:3); 'made a breach' = broke out (Exodus 19:22; the death of Uzza, 1 Chronicles 13:10); the fault was the cart instead of the shoulders (v.15; Numbers 4:15) — the sanctity of divine institutions.
Gill corrects the AV supplement: the sense is 'ye were not there at first,' the Levites not employed as was their duty when the ark came to Obededom's house.
Keil ties the self-consecration to the bearing that follows (Numbers 7:9; the poles of Numbers 13:23, the 'bars' of Exodus 25:13) and passes straight on to David's charge to appoint the singers.
Gill: they sanctified themselves as ordered and 'in the manner they should' — the manner that had been wanting at the first attempt.

Keil grounds the carrying in Numbers 7:9 and Exodus 25:13; Ellicott renders 'with their shoulder, with the poles upon themselves' and notes the verse anticipates the procession told in full from v.25 — a common Hebrew summary device.
Clarke pictures the staves through the rings resting on the shoulders, the ark borne like a sedan on its poles (Numbers 4:15).
Gill, with Maimonides, gives the reason for carrying by hand and not by wagon — the ark's honour, and lest its structure be damaged.

themes: borne on the shoulders with staves

schools.summary: Ellicott refers 'sounding' to the clashing cymbals alone (cf. Psalms 150:5; 2 Chronicles 5:13) and names the nebalim and kinnoroth; Keil describes the three instruments — psaltery, lute, and cymbals — appointed to accompany the song.

Matthew Henry: David the prophet instituted instrumental praise by divine direction (1 Peter 4:10 on using gifts), though the New Testament keeps psalm-singing without appointing church-music (Hebrews 9:10).
Gill: David spoke to the chief of the Levites, the six princes, to appoint their brethren as singers.

Ellicott: Heman, Asaph, and Ethan (Jeduthun) were the precentors of the three choirs, one from each Levite house — Heman of Kohath, Asaph of Gershom, Ethan of Merari (1 Chronicles 6:33ff.); Keil describes the choirs they formed.
Clarke names them the three chief musicians of David's day (1 Chronicles 6:31).
Gill marks Heman as son of Joel and grandson of Samuel the prophet.

Ellicott: fourteen second-rank minstrels (cf. 'his second Zechariah,' 1 Chronicles 16:5); 'Ben' is not a name but the Hebrew for 'son,' a father's name lost or a scribal slip (LXX omits); Shemiramoth resembles Assyrian Sammurramat/Semiramis; 'Jaaziel' a slip for 'Jeiel.'
Wesley: the second degree ranks below the three of v.17, and the porters, though doorkeepers, were also skilled in music.
Gill: 'the second degree' are those next to the three chief musicians in office and skill.

debates.question: Is 'Ben' the name of a singer?

sides.position: not a name but the Hebrew for 'son'; a father's name has dropped out or a scribe erred (LXX omits it)
a singer once counted, later absent (sickened or died), perhaps the 'Ben' of the psalm-title

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'cymbals of bronze for clashing,' makes the three chiefs lead and accentuate the music, and lays out the marching order (master-singers, two bands of seven, Chenaniah, two warders, seven trumpeting priests); Keil describes the three choirs by their instruments.
Gill: these were the three principal singers, set apart for the cymbals.

Ellicott takes 'on Alamoth' as 'after the mode of maidens,' soprano pitch (heading of Psalms 46); Keil notes 'Alamoth' and 'Sheminith' mark different keys, obscure now for want of Hebrew music; Aziel = the Jaaziel of v.18.
Clarke reports 'Alamoth' as virgins/women singers, or 'secret things'/prophetic hymns (Vulgate); Wesley confesses its meaning — an instrument, tune, note, or part — is now lost, as is 'Sheminith.'
Gill counts eight of the second degree, notes 'Ben' has dropped out with Azaziah put in his room (v.21), and the fancy linking Ben to the psalm-title 'upon Muthlabben.'

sides.position: 'after the mode of maidens' — soprano compass or pitch
virgins or women singers to be accompanied, or 'secret things'/prophetic hymns (Vulgate)
a lost term — an instrument, tune, or part whose meaning is now unknown

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'with lutes in the bass,' literally 'after the mode of the eighth,' an octave below the tenor; 'to excel' = to lead, to precent (heading of Psalms 6).

Clarke gathers the guesses — an octave-instrument, eight strings, hours of the day, or the eighth band of strong voices; Wesley reads 'to excel' as the instrument's excellency or a raised voice, and owns the term is now lost, but affirms David instituted this praise by divine direction.
Gill: these six were appointed to sound.

sides.position: 'in the bass,' an octave below the tenor
an octave-instrument, an eight-stringed instrument, or the eighth band of deep voices

schools.summary: Clarke and Wesley make Chenaniah the master singer and precentor, who gave the key and time and taught the choirs when to lift or change their voices (with the LXX's 'leader of the songs').

Ellicott dissents: 'captain of the Levites in the bearing,' skilled in the rules of carrying the ark — grounded in the context, Chenaniah's non-musical role at 1 Chronicles 26:29, the sense of massa (Numbers 4:19; 2 Chronicles 35:3), and 'master of the carriage' at v.27.
Gill leans away from the song-master reading, allowing that 'the chief for the song' were rather Heman, Asaph, and Ethan.

debates.question: Was Chenaniah master of the song or master of the ark's bearing?

sides.position: master singer and precentor, giving the key and time
captain of the Levites over the carriage of the ark, skilled in the rules of bearing

schools.summary: Ellicott glosses 'doorkeepers' as warders (the 'porters' of the earlier list), with two before the ark and two behind (Obededom and Jehiah, v.24), to prevent any unauthorized approach; Keil sets them in the marching order leading to v.25.
Wesley: they kept the door of the tent and guarded the ark along the way from the throng and the touch of profane hands.
Gill offers three settings for the office, preferring that they went before the ark as it was carried, to keep the people from pressing or gazing.

Ellicott counts seven priestly trumpeters, the silver clarions of Numbers 10:2 (cf. 1 Chronicles 16:6), perhaps before the ark as at Jericho (Joshua 6:4); and doubts the doorkeepers Obededom and Jehiah are the minstrels of vv.18, 21 (order of march, 'Jeiel' vs 'Jehiah,' recurring names, 1 Chronicles 16:38).
Gill: the silver trumpets, blown when the camps and the ark set forward.

debates.question: Is the doorkeeper Obededom the same as the minstrel Obededom?

sides.position: probably two different men, given the marching order, the differing names, and the frequency of recurring names

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'it was David and the elders... who were walking to bring up the ark,' notes Samuel (2 Samuel 6:12) omits the elders and captains, and that the special title 'ark of the covenant of the LORD' (cf. Joshua 3:3) may point to another source; Keil joins the verse to the sacrifice following.
Matthew Henry: take notice of divine providence even in things within our natural powers — the trembling Levites were helped and their faith strengthened, all sufficiency being from God (Acts 26:22).
Gill: the king, the principal men, and the officers of the militia gathered to fetch the ark from Obededom's house.

themes: the ark of the covenant title

schools.summary: Keil sets the offering after the journey, where 2 Samuel 6:13 has it at the start (six paces); Ellicott weighs whether the two are one sacrifice or two, rejecting the 'six-paces' priests as incredible and judging the Chronicler to paraphrase Samuel, giving the victims exactly (Numbers 23:1).
Clarke: seeing no breach as upon Uzza, they offered in gratitude; Wesley: God 'helped' by a comfortable sign of his presence, and in all religious exercises we must derive help from heaven.
Gill: 'helped' means not bodily strength but to carry with cheerfulness and without error, unlike the first attempt from Kirjathjearim.

debates.question: Do Chronicles and Samuel record the same sacrifice or two different ones?

sides.position: one and the same sacrifice, the Chronicler paraphrasing Samuel and giving the victims exactly
distinct offerings — Samuel at the outset (six paces), Chronicles after the journey's end
a bullock and a fatling slain at every six paces by priests along the route, with a final sacrifice at the arrival

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil judge 'clothed with a robe of byssus' a corruption or alteration of Samuel's 'danced with all his might' (2 Samuel 6:14) — the Aramaic mekurbal (Daniel 3:21) for the rare mekarker; Ellicott again renders 'master of the song' as 'chief of the bearing' (v.22) and the me'il as a rank-garment (Job 29:14).
Clarke describes the byssus as spun from the beard of the Pinna Magna, unknown in Israel before David; Wesley marks the repeated ephod as unusual for a non-Levite to wear.
Gill: David, though a king, was clothed as the singers, being among them.

debates.question: Did the original text say David 'danced' or that he was 'clothed' with linen?

sides.position: 'danced with all his might' (Samuel) is original; 'clothed with a robe of byssus' arose from it by a change of letters
the received 'clothed with fine linen' stands and describes David's priestly garb

schools.summary: Keil notes the verse is enlarged over 2 Samuel 6:5 by naming the instruments; Ellicott renders 'all Israel were bringing up the ark,' corrects 'cornet' to trumpet, and would read 'with clanging cymbals, with harps and lutes' (cf. Psalms 150:3).
Gill passes over the verse briefly, referring to the parallel.

themes: all Israel brought up the ark

schools.summary: Ellicott reads the verse as a modernised form of 2 Samuel 6:16, with 'dancing and playing' (dancing joined to song and instruments, Jeremiah 30:19; 1 Samuel 18:6) for Samuel's obsolete words, and Michal's scorn because David seemed to forget his royal honour, dancing like a woman; Keil notes the Chronicler explains a few words and refers the rest to Samuel.
Gill sets Michal at the palace window despising David, referring to the exchange at 2 Samuel 6:20.
Clarke points to 2 Samuel 6:20, where David's conduct is vindicated and Michal's disgrace hinted, left to the reader.

themes: the house of Saul versus the heart of David
